data.title: 10 Best CEFs This Month: Average Yield Of 8.7% (January 2026)
data.description:
Introduction:

During the last two months of 2025, the market was mostly flat and traded in a tight range between S&P 500 (SPX) levels 6600 and 6900. However, the first two weeks of 2026 have been decent so far. It also made new highs a couple of times in the first week of this year, and the SPX level 7000 is not out of reach. The bull market is widely expected to continue in 2026, but there are a lot of things happening all around, especially geopolitically. We may have a new tariff tantrum that is just starting with Europe in focus over the issue of Greenland. The market can have a big negative swing in a hurry, especially when the valuations are so rich. However, the new theme for 2026 is that the foreign markets are expected to perform better (than the U.S. markets), similar to the trend that started in 2025. The main reason may be that they are still much cheaper in terms of valuations compared to the S&P500. So, it may be wise to diversify somewhat and have some allocations to foreign developed markets, and possibly a small allocation to the emerging markets.

Lastly (but not the least), the AI rally appears to be in the bubble territory, due to overspending and over-borrowing, but as they say, bubbles can last longer than most people think. Even then, it is certainly a reason to adopt a cautious approach.

The further progress and direction of the market may also depend on the actions of the Fed. You can see the current odds of the Fed's next rate cut here on the CME FedWatchTool website.

All that being said, it is always hard to find anything that has both the quality and the low valuation, so buying the best of the income investments regularly in small quantities is the key. As always, investors have to tread carefully amid all the uncertainties.

Why CEFs?

The broader market, as represented by the S&P500, provides a dismal level of yield of less than 1.10%. Income investors and retirees can't survive on that level of income unless they have a very large pool of capital. Also, the method of raising income by selling shares is generally very stressful and full of pitfalls, and that's why it is not recommended. Alternatively, one can find solid blue-chip individual dividend stocks that pay much higher dividends than the S&P 500. They can also provide market-matching growth on a longer-term basis. To identify such stocks, we also publish a monthly article on the '5 Best DGI stocks'. You can find the most recent one here.

However, with CEFs, we can take the income to the next level. That said, they generally come with higher risks (discussed later), so we believe that an allocation of 20% to 30% to a diversified CEF portfolio should be good enough for most income investors. In summary, these investments should be used primarily for income, not growth.

We publish this monthly article on CEFs (Closed-end funds) to provide our readers with a select list of 10 CEFs that, as a group, provide wide diversification in terms of underlying assets, solid long-term performance at least on a relative basis, and are trading at decent valuations. Above all, the income goal for the group is to provide a yield of at least 8% or higher.

This month, as a group, the selected 10 best funds are offering an average distribution rate of 8.70% (as of 01/16/2026). Besides excellent distributions, these funds have had an excellent record over the long term and collectively returned 17.7%, 12.2%, and 13.3% (annualized) in the last three, five years, and 10 years, respectively. The average discount (to NAV) is attractive, at -7.5%. Since this is a monthly series, some selections may overlap from month to month.

Why Is It Important To Have A Diversified CEF Portfolio?

We believe a well-diversified CEF portfolio should contain at least 10 CEFs, preferably from different asset classes. It's also advisable to build the portfolio over a period rather than invest in one lump sum. If you were to invest in one CEF every month for a year, you would have a well-diversified CEF portfolio by the year's end. What we provide here every month is a list of ten probable candidates for further research. A CEF portfolio can be an important component of the overall portfolio strategy. One should preferably have a DGI portfolio as the foundation, and the CEF portfolio could be used to boost the income level to the desired level. How much should one allocate to CEFs? Each investor needs to answer this question himself/herself based on their personal situation and factors like the size of the portfolio, income needs, risk appetite, or risk tolerance.

Criteria to Shortlist:

Criteria

Brings down the number of funds to...

Reason for the Criteria

Baseline expense < 2.5% and Avg. Daily Volume > 10,000

Approx. 435 Funds

We do not want funds that charge excessive fees. Also, we want funds that have fair liquidity.

Market capitalization> 100 Million

Approx. 400 Funds

We do not want funds that are too small.

Track record/ History longer than five years (inception date 2016 or earlier)

Approx. 375 Funds

We want funds that have a reasonably long track record.

Discount/Premium < +7%

Approx. 350 Funds

We do not want to pay too high a premium; we want bigger discounts.

Distribution (dividend) Rate > 5%

Approx. 250-290 Funds

The current distribution (income) is reasonably high.

5-Year Annualized Return on NAV > 0% AND/ OR

3-Year Annualized Return on NAV >0%

Approx. 200-250 Funds

We want funds with a reasonably good past track record in maintaining their NAVs.

After we applied the above criteria this month, we are generally left with around 250 funds (plus or minus 5%) on our list. From here on, we apply our other criteria to narrow the list.

Note: All tables in this article have been created by the author (unless explicitly specified). Most of the data in this article is sourced from Cefconnect.com, Cefa.com, and Morningstar.com.

Narrowing Down To 50 Funds:

To narrow down the number of funds to a more manageable count, we will shortlist ten funds based on each of the following criteria. Thereafter, we will apply certain qualitative criteria to each fund and rank them to select the top five.

At this stage, we also eliminate certain funds with substantially negative NAV returns for both three-year and five-year periods.

Seven Broad Criteria:

  • Excess discount/premium (explained below).
  • Distribution rate.
  • Return on NAV, last three years (medium-term).
  • Return on NAV, last five years (long term).
  • Coverage ratio.
  • Excess return over distributions.
  • The total weight (calculated up to this point).

Excess Discount/Premium

We certainly like funds that are offering large discounts (not premiums) to their NAVs. But what's important is to look at the "excess discount/premium" and not the absolute value. We want to see the discount (or premium) relative to their past record, say, a 52-week average.

Excess Discount/Premium = Current Discount/Premium (Minus) 52-Wk Avg. Discount/ Premium.

For more explanation of the excess discount and z-scores, please refer to our blog post (link provided at the beginning of the article).

We sort our list (of approx. 250 funds) on the "excess discount/premium" in descending order. For this criterion, the lower the value, the better it is. So, we select the top 10 funds (with the most negative values) from this sorted list.

(All data as of 01/16/2026)

Table 1:

High Current Distribution Rate

After all, most investors invest in CEF funds for their juicy distributions. Even though we should not chase yield, at this stage, we want to give them a fair representation in our list and subject them to a more rigorous criterion later on. We sorted our list on the current distribution rate (descending order, highest at the top) and selected the top 10 funds from this sorted list.

Table 2:

Medium-Term Return on NAV (last three years)

We then sort our list on a three-year return on NAV (in descending order, highest at the top) and select the top 10 funds. For this reason, we make a shortlist based on the total score and keep only a few of them on this list.

Table 3:

Five-Year Annualized Return on NAV

We then sorted our list on the five-year return on NAV (in descending order, highest at the top) and selected the top 10 funds.

Table 4:

Coverage Ratio (Distributions Vs. Earnings)

The coverage ratio is derived by dividing the earnings per share by the distribution amount for a specific period. Please note that in some cases, the coverage ratio may be a bit inaccurate since the "earnings per share" may be three to six months old. But in most cases, it's fairly accurate. Furthermore, the coverage ratio is not critical for pure equity funds as they generate very little dividend income, and they cover most of their distributions from capital gains. However, we sorted our list by coverage ratio and selected the top 10 funds.

Table 5:

Excess Return Over Distribution

If a fund provides a very high distribution but low returns over 3, 5, or 10-year periods, it does not help the long-term interests of the investor. In simple terms, the fund is just overpaying the distributions and hurting the NAV in the long term. That is why we calculate a factor we like to call "excess return." This excess return is what is provided by the fund over and above the distribution rate over a specified time period. We calculate it by subtracting the distribution rate from the average of the three-year and five-year NAV returns. In this list, we included the top 10 funds.

Table 6:

Total Weight (Quality Score) Calculated Up to This Point

Note: The Total Weight calculation is not fully completed at this point since we have not considered the 10-year NAV return. However, we select the top 15 names based on current calculations.

Table 7:

From the selections so far, we have 75 funds in total (tables 1-7).

We will need to see if there are any duplicates among them. In our current list of 75 funds, there were 26 duplicates, meaning there are funds that appeared more than once. The following names appear twice (or more):

Appeared twice: ADX, AIO, EDF, EOD, GAM, HYT, and XFLT (7 duplicates).

Appeared three times: CSQ, EIC, EMO, PDX, and TWN (10 duplicates).

Appeared four times: EOS, GGN, and JCE (9 duplicates).

Once we remove 26 duplicate rows, we are left with 49 (75- 26) funds.

Note: It may be worthwhile to mention here that just because a fund has appeared multiple times does not necessarily make it an attractive candidate. Sometimes, a fund may appear multiple times simply for the wrong reasons, like a high current discount, a high excess discount, or a very high distribution rate that may not be sustainable. However, during the second filtering stage, it may not score well on the overall quality score due to other factors, like a poor track record. That said, if a fund has appeared four times or more, it may be worth a second look.

Narrowing Down to Just 10 Funds:

In our list of funds, we may already have some of the best probable candidates. However, so far, they have been selected based on one (or two) criteria that each may be good at. That's not nearly enough. So, we will apply a combination of criteria by applying weights to eight factors to calculate the total quality score and filter out the best ones.

We will apply weights to each of the eight criteria:

Criteria

Max. Weight

Comment

Baseline expense

5.0

Current distribution rate

7.5

Excess discount/premium

5.0

3-YR NAV return

5.0

5-YR NAV return

5.0

10-YR NAV return or 10-YR Total return

5.0

If less than ten years of history, an average of three and five-year returns is taken.

Excess NAV return over distribution rate

5.0

Adjusted Coverage Ratio

7.5

This factor is adjusted for certain factors. The adjustment is performed based on the type of fund, such as equity and sector funds.

Once we have calculated the weights, we combine them to calculate the "Total Combined Weight," also called the "Quality Score."

The sorted list (spreadsheet) of 49 funds on the "combined total weight or quality score" is attached here.

File-for-export_-_10_Best_CEFs_-_Jan_2026.xlsx

10-Positions Portfolio of The Month:

Before we get to the final ten, we will narrow down our list to 20 names. So, we sorted our table based on the sector and then the quality score. Furthermore, in this round of selections, we mostly pick one or two top funds from each sector (based on quality score). There is a bit of subjective judgment, as we tend to prioritize funds that pay regular and consistent distributions on a monthly or quarterly basis. We also weed out any fund that may have a very poor track record in terms of NAV performance. Funds that may have inconsistent dividends (even if they are high) generally do not make it to our top list. Furthermore, be aware that many times, single-country funds score high in our rankings, but most of them pay variable dividends on an annual or semi-annual basis. In addition, being single-country funds, they can be inherently riskier since their future returns are tied to just one country, be it economic, regulatory, or geopolitical factors. For these reasons, they generally do not make it to our top 5 lists, but they do appear on our list of 20. Individual investors could consider them based on their goals.

Here's the list of the top 20 selections (from different asset classes) in the order of sector:

Table 8:

10-Best-CEFs of the Month

In the end, if we only want to select ten funds, we will need to apply our subjective judgment to some degree. While we narrow down this list to ten names, we would be mindful to keep the list as diversified as possible in terms of sectors and asset classes. Since this step is somewhat subjective, the choice would differ from person to person. Nonetheless, here are the selections for this month, based on our perspective (in order of Quality Score):

Here's the list of the top 10 selections this month (in order of sector):

(EOS), (AIO), (EIC), (EOD), (PDX), (JCE), (CSQ), (RNP), (PEO), and (CCD).

Table-9:

Some information about the selections:

We will only briefly describe the selected funds. Since there are 10 of them, we will not be providing a very detailed analysis of them.

Here are some details on many of our selections:

(EOS) Eaton Vance Enhanced Equity Income II:

When it comes to covered call funds, it is hard to beat Eaton Vance funds. Also, EOS is one of the best funds in its category. Moreover, its high exposure to the technology sector has definitely helped the fund to shine in the last 10 years. It currently holds only about 50 companies and overwrites (sells options) roughly 50% of its portfolio to generate income and reduce volatility. The distribution yield is attractive at 8.1%, and currently offers a decent discount of -7%. The fund uses no leverage, and its expenses are comparable to those of its competitors. The past performance record is solid, and it has returned 25%, 11%, and 14.8% (annualized) over the last 3, 5, and 10-year periods.

(AIO) Virtus AI & Tech Opp Fund:

This is a fund focused on AI (Artificial Intelligence) and technology companies. But it comes with a unique flavor of combining both the equity and convertible securities, as well as some bonds, based on market conditions. Please note the fund has a limited term until 2031 (extendable by 18 months). The fund also uses modest leverage and has performed exceedingly well in the last three years. It provides a decent distribution yield of just under 8% and is currently trading at a decent discount of roughly -7%. This fund was included last month as well, and the price has gone up somewhat since then, so the yield is slightly lower.

(EIC) Eagle Point Income Co:

EIC is probably the riskiest fund in this selection of 10 funds. The fund uses high leverage of 31% in the form of preferred stock. The fund primarily invests in junior debt tranches of CLOs (Collateralized Loan Obligations). In addition, it may invest up to 35% of total assets in CLO equity securities and related securities.

Due to the decline in interest rates, similar to its peers, the fund has been reducing its distribution amount. The monthly distribution has come down from $0.20 a share to $0.11, a 45% reduction. This has caused the price to fall during most of 2025, and that has continued this year, but the pace has slowed down. So, it has been brutal for someone who acquired the shares prior to 2024. However, the yield is still very attractive for new buyers at 11.8%, in spite of the lower distribution amount. The discount has also widened to -15.6%. With reduced distribution, the current distribution is fully covered by the NII (net investment income). However, on a full nine-month basis (January to September), it only covered to the extent of 75%, as the distribution was much higher at the beginning of last year. We think that after significant correction, the price and yield are attractive. However, it is quite likely that the distribution amount may further fall as the interest rates decline even more, so this fund should only be accumulated in multiple lots on a dollar-cost-average basis.

(EOD) Allspring Global Dividend Opportunity Fund:

The international funds of all types have done well during the year 2025, and that trend is continuing in 2026. This fund is selected to provide exposure to global equity as well as fixed income. The fund is invested roughly 80% in global equities with a focus on dividends, and the rest in fixed income (18%) and cash equivalents (2%). The equity portion is invested in 70-80 companies spread across many sectors and segments. The fund also employs an options strategy on a portion of the equity portfolio to enhance income and to generate gains on call options. The fund also uses a modest leverage of just under 15%.

The allocation to the U.S. is roughly 65% of the total, and the rest is invested in countries such as the U.K., France, Japan, China, Germany, Italy, Canada, Taiwan, and Brazil. The largest sector is information Technology at 25%, followed by Financials and Communications.

(PDX) PIMCO Dynamic Income Strategy Fund:

As we did last month, we are including PDX, one of PIMCO's newest but popular funds, in our top 10. This fund regularly appears near the top in multiple categories. The reason it stands out is that it has performed remarkably well in the last three and five years. The fund is only about six years old, so it does not have a long history. Some of its past performance can be attributed to its timing of inception (in 2019) and initial allocation to the energy sector. In fact, this fund was known as "PIMCO Energy & Tactical Credit Opportunities fund (ticker - NRGX)" until Nov.2023. Since the beginning of 2024, PIMCO has decided to diversify the fund from an energy-focused fund to a mix of energy and multi-sector Credit/Bonds. Unlike other PIMCO funds, its distribution yield is a bit low but has gone up significantly to over 8%, due to the ongoing correction in price. PIMCO has aggressively raised the dividends in the last two years and has also paid special year-end distributions. The fund is currently offering an attractive discount of nearly -10% compared to premiums on other similar PIMCO funds.

(JCE) Nuveen Core Equity Alpha

Under the U.S. equity category, we are selecting JCE this month, though General American Investors (GAM) and Adams Diversified Equity Fund (ADX) are also equally attractive. That said, for long-term holding, we would prefer ADX out of the three.

The fund pays quarterly, and its distribution amount is usually consistent, unlike GAM. JCE’s current yield is attractive at 8.01%, and the discount is decent at -6% (better than long-term averages).

The primary investment objective of the fund is to seek capital appreciation, with income only a secondary objective. The fund invests mostly in large-capitalized core stocks with growth potential at reasonable valuations. The fund does not use leverage, but writes call options on a portion of the portfolio. It holds roughly 110 equity stocks. The fund has shown excellent performance of 22.4%, 14.1%, and 13.3% over the last 3, 5, and 10 years.

(CSQ) Calamos Strategic Total Return Fund:

This fund is a hybrid fund and invests primarily in a diversified portfolio of equities, convertible securities, and high-yield corporate bonds. Nearly 66% of CSQ's capital is invested in equities, and the rest is in convertibles, bonds, and preferred securities. Also, the equity is a bit concentrated in tech (but lower than the S&P500) and other growth-oriented stocks, but it does represent other sectors reasonably well. Performance-wise, since 2005, it has nearly matched the returns of the S&P500, assuming all distributions were reinvested. With 1/3rd of the portfolio in fixed income, we would expect CSQ to underperform the S&P500, but it achieves similar performance by using significant leverage of roughly 30%. The leverage increases the volatility and drawdowns somewhat higher than those of the S&P500. It recently increased the distribution amount, and the current yield is quite decent at 7.55%.

(RNP) Cohen & Steers REIT & Preferred Inc Fund:

This is a hybrid fund and provides nearly identical exposure to real estate equity, mostly REITs, and the other half to preferred and fixed income securities. The fund also uses significant leverage of over 30%. The real estate sector had a tough few years due to the elevated interest rates. But with the expectations of continued decline in interest rates, the sector may experience a recovery in 2026, as has been the case during the first two weeks of 2026. The fund currently offers a moderate discount of roughly -4.6% and a decent distribution yield of 8.1%.

(PEO) Adams Natural Resources Fund:

If you like to own some of the large-cap energy majors at a discount, you can't beat the PEO fund offered by the Adams group. It is a very nice fund to own the major oil companies at a discount (currently hovering around -9%). This fund provides a diversified exposure to large energy companies. At the same time, there is some over-concentration at the top, given the fact that the two oil majors, Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Chevron (CVX), together represent nearly 37% of the total assets. In addition, it currently provides an attractive yield of 8.4%. Please keep in mind that this is an energy sector-specific fund, and hence, one should allocate accordingly.

(CCD) Calamos Dynamic Convertible and Income:

The Fund primarily invests in a portfolio of convertible securities, debt securities, and other income-producing securities, including some equities. Investments include common and preferred stocks, investment-grade and below-investment-grade bonds, loans, equity-linked notes, and floating-rate securities. It also uses high leverage exceeding 30%. This fund was selected last month as well. Since then, it has appreciated somewhat, and the yield has gone down a bit. But it still pays a yield of 10.4% on current prices. The distribution is paid monthly. The shares are trading at a small but insignificant premium and compare favorably with their very high premiums over the last 12 months and 3 years. The lower interest rates expected in the future should help this fund. However, on the negative side, there is some chance that the management may reduce the distribution payout as it has a negative income from operations (NII + realized and unrealized capital gains). So, investors need to be somewhat cautious and use due diligence.

CEF-Specific Investment Risks:

It goes without saying that CEFs generally have some additional risks. This section is specifically relevant for investors who are new to CEF investing, but in general, all CEF investors should be aware of it. For more details on these specific risks, please read our blog post on CEFs.

  • Use of high leverage and high fees
  • High volatility (compared to the broader market)
  • Premium over NAVs (market price higher than the NAV)
  • Asset-specific concentration risk.

Concluding Thoughts:

The broader market, as represented by the S&P500, provides a dismal yield of less than 1.20%. Many income investors and retirees can't survive on that kind of income. For income investors, we recommend building a core bucket of DGI (Dividend growth) stocks and supplementing it with a basket of CEFs to increase the overall income.

We use our screening process to highlight the best closed-end funds for investment each month. Our filtering process demands that our selections have an above-average long-term performance record, offer an average of roughly 8% distributions, and are relatively cheaper with reasonable discounts. We have also provided a larger list of ten CEFs from many different asset classes.

This month, as a group, the selected 10 best funds are offering an average distribution rate of 8.70% (as of 01/16/2026). Besides excellent distributions, these funds have had an excellent record over the long term and collectively returned 17.7%, 12.2%, and 13.3% (annualized) in the last three, five years, and 10 years, respectively. The average discount (to NAV) is attractive, at -7.5%. Since this is a monthly series, some selections may overlap from month to month.

Please note that these selections are based on our proprietary rating system and are dynamic in nature. So, they can change from month to month (or even week to week). At the same time, some funds can repeat from one month to the next if they remain attractive over an extended period. Furthermore, note that if a fund has not made it to our list, it does not mean it is not good. It could be because they may not be attractively priced at this time or trading at a premium while running our filtering process.

data.title: Graphite Miners News For The Month Of January 2026
data.description:
Welcome to the January edition of the graphite miners news.

The past month saw China battery related flake graphite spot prices mixed with little change. Prices remain very depressed.

Graphite price news

During the past 30 days the China graphite flake-194 EXW spot price was flat. The China graphite flake +195 EXW spot price was -2.59%. Note that 94-97% is considered best suited for use in batteries; it is then upgraded to 99.9% purity to make “spherical” graphite used in Li-ion batteries. The spherical graphite 99.95% min EXW China price was up 1.12% the past 30 days. Note: Graphite electrode prices were slightly lower.

Note: SMM lists the current flake-194 graphite spot price at US$373/t including VAT, generally flat from US$369 last month.

Graphite demand and supply forecast charts

The IEA Global critical Minerals Outlook 2024 report forecasts graphite demand and supply pipeline to 2040 (source)

Trend Investing v IEA demand forecast for EV metals (IEA)

2021 IEA forecast growth in demand for selected minerals from clean energy technologies by scenario, 2040 relative to 2020 - Increases Of Lithium 13x to 42x, Graphite 8x to 25x, Cobalt 6x to 21x, Nickel 7x to 19x, Manganese 3x to 8x, Rare Earths 3x to 7x, And Copper 2x to 3x

Graphite market news

On January 7 Fastmarkets reported:

Synthetic graphite gains ground as natural graphite struggles with weak demand...The synthetic side of the market is gaining steam going into 2026, as its performance characteristics and current near-cost parity with natural materials make synthetic an increasingly attractive choice for battery makers.

On January 19 Investing News Network reported:

Graphite Market Forecast: Top Trends for Graphite in 2026. Oversupply, trade friction and China’s continued dominance defined the global graphite market in 2025. Looking ahead, long-term demand tied to electric vehicles and energy storage continues to drive growth...Prices hit multi-year lows as a US investigation into Chinese anode imports highlighted the vulnerability of the electric vehicle (EV) supply chain, with tariffs and anti-dumping duties creating uncertainty for North American producers. Although natural graphite output has risen from 966,000 metric tons in 2020 to 1.6 million metric tons in 2024, China accounts for nearly all recent supply growth and also dominates refining...

in flake graphite you have competition with synthetic graphite... “Flake graphite, you'll see that that price is going up despite the oversupplied market, and that is because to meet that rising demand, there needs to be more supply coming online, and a lot of that supply coming online is high cost. So that's going to push up the price support, basically, gradually through time,” Webb said...Today, more than 90 percent of battery-grade anode material is sourced from China...Cost remains the central obstacle. Building an anode plant in North America can cost three to 10 times more than in China, while customers remain reluctant to pay a premium.

Graphite miners news

Flake Graphite producers

We have not covered the following Chinese graphite producers but they should also be considered (Ningbo Shanshan Co Ltd (Shanshan Technology) [SHA:600884], BTR New Energy Materials [BJSE:835185], Jiangxi Zichen (private), Kaijin New Energy Technology Corp Ltd (private), Shijiazhuang Shangtai Technology Co. Ltd. [SHE:001301], and Zhongke [SHE:300035]).

Syrah Resources Limited [ASX:SYR][GR:3S7](OTCPK:SYAAF)(OTC:SRHYY)

Syrah Resources Limited owns the Balama graphite mine in Mozambique. Syrah is also a vertically integrated producer of natural graphite Active Anode Material (“AAM”) at their Vidalia facility, Louisiana, USA.

On January 19 Syrah Resources announced:

Tesla offtake alleged default cure date extended...the parties have extended the Cure Date to 16 March 2026 and are closely collaborating to cure the alleged default.

Tirupati Graphite [LSE:TGR]

On January 12 Tirupati Graphite announced:

Sub-Division of Ordinary Shares. At the GM, inter alia, a resolution was passed which approved the sub-division of each existing ordinary share of £0.025 into one new ordinary share of £0.01 (a "New Ordinary Share") and one deferred share of £0.015 (a "Deferred Share") (each such Deferred Share having no voting or dividend rights and effectively being worthless). The New Ordinary Shares will become effective on 14 January 2026, however the Company's listing remains suspended. Further updates regarding, inter alia, the publication of the delayed annual report and accounts for the year ended 31 March 2025, together with delayed half year financial statements to 30 September 2025, the publication of an FCA- approved prospectus, and the lifting of the suspension will be made in due course.

Northern Graphite [TSXV:NGC][FRA:ONG] (OTCQX:NGPHF)

Northern Graphite purchased from Imerys the Lac des Iles producing graphite mine in Quebec and the Okanjande graphite deposit/Okorusu processing plant in Namibia. They also own the Bissett Creek graphite project located 100km east of North Bay, Ontario, Canada and close to major roads and infrastructure. The Company has completed an NI 43-101 Bankable final Feasibility Study and received its major environmental permit.

On January 14 Northern Graphite announced:

Northern Graphite and Obeikan Investment Group sign Term Sheet for US$200 million Battery Anode Material Plant in Kingdom of Saudi Arabia.

  • Joint Venture Company to be 51% owned by Obeikan and 49% by Northern.
  • Project debt financing to be sourced from Saudi government finance agencies, local and global commercial banks.
  • Phase I BAM production capacity of 25,000 tonnes per year forecast for 2028; scalable expansion to meet growing global demand.
  • Advanced discussions underway with global battery manufacturers regarding long-term offtake agreements for the initial 25,000 tpy of production.
  • Northern and JVCo to conclude long-term offtake agreement for purchase of up to 50,000 tpy of graphite concentrate from Northern’s Okanjande mine in Namibia.
  • Project aligned with Saudi Arabia’s Vision 2030 and accelerating demand for secure, non-Chinese graphite anode supply chains...

NextSource Materials Inc. [TSX:NEXT] [GR:1JW] (NSRCF)

NextSource Materials Inc. 100% owns the Molo Graphite Mine in Madagascar. The Company also has the Green Giant Vanadium Project on the same property.

On January 7 Next Source Materials announced:

Arrival of first equipment shipment in UAE for its Battery Anode Facility and significant progress on front-end engineering and design.

On January 12 Next Source Materials announced:

NextSource Materials executes Term Sheets with strategic investors and local capital partners to advance towards Final Investment Decision for its proposed UAE Battery Anode Facility...The term sheets propose an equity investment at the project level in the BAF project under which new strategic partners would potentially acquire an interest of up to 50% in the UAE BAF and subsequently fund their pro rata share of Phase 1 capital costs.

Leading Edge Materials [TSXV:LEM] (LEMIF)

Leading Edge Materials Corp. is a Canadian company focused on becoming a sustainable supplier of a range of critical materials. Leading Edge Materials’ flagship asset is the Woxna Graphite Project and processing plant in central Sweden. The company also owns the Norra Karr REE project, and the 51% of the Bihor Sud Nickel-Cobalt exploration stage project in Romania.

No news for the month.

data.title: Full Portfolio Review: 5%+ Yield And 5%+ Dividend Growth
data.description:
I want to do something I have never done before on Seeking Alpha: a full review of my investment portfolio.

I'm constantly reviewing various parts of my portfolio, and I do a full review of my portfolio about once a year. I just don't share it with the world by writing about it. Up until recently, I've had the mindset that sharing my full portfolio is self-important and that nobody cares or will make changes to their own portfolios based on what I do.

But recently, I've realized that I personally enjoy seeing what other investors, specifically those focused on passive income and dividend growth, are doing with their portfolios.

Usually my reactions are something along the lines of, "That's too much weight in BDCs" or "why ruin a perfectly good portfolio with Bitcoin" or "you own that stock?!" But sometimes I garner some interesting ideas, and at the very least, looking at others' portfolios spurs thought about how I want to allocate capital in my own.

So what the heck. Let's do it.

In what follows, I'll give an overview of how I think about portfolio construction as an income-oriented dividend growth investor, then I'll slice and dice my portfolio in various ways to break it down, and I'll finish with my buy list today.

My hope is that this exercise is helpful to you in some way.

The Galley Ship Metaphor

Readers of my past articles may know that I like to envision my dividend portfolio like a Medieval galley ship -- the kind with both oars and sails.

I discussed that metaphor at length in these articles:

  • The Galley Ship Portfolio: Allocating Capital For Perpetual Dividend Growth
  • How I've Built A Monster Passive Income Portfolio

In short, the rowers/oars are the high-quality compounders that are capable of steady, indefinite dividend growth.

The sails are the high-yielding securities, whether preferred equities, business development companies, closed-end funds, or something else.

The rowers generate dividend growth, thereby intrinsically growing portfolio income, while the sails generate high current income, thereby facilitating extrinsic growth in portfolio income via selective reinvestment.

The ultimate goal is not primarily to grow the biggest possible portfolio value, nor to generate the highest total returns, but rather to create the biggest, safest, and fastest growing passive income stream as possible. If I've accomplished that goal, strong total returns and a growing portfolio value should be second-order effects.

For someone in or near retirement, the goal might simply be to safely maximize passive income. I wrote about that last week in "The Income Quintet: 5 Pillars of Safe High Yield."

But I'm in my mid-30s and don't need the income today. All investment income is getting reinvested. The question, for me, is how to optimally balance dividend growth and yield to maximize portfolio income many years into the future.

Imagine standing at the top of a snowy hill in wintertime. If your goal is to create the biggest snowball by the time it gets to the bottom of the hill, should you start with a small snowball that you can roll faster (low-yielding stocks with fast dividend growth)? Or would it be better to start with a big snowball that you can't initially roll as fast but that gathers snow faster going downhill due to its larger circumference (high-yielding stocks with low dividend growth and more income to reinvest)?

I've settled for somewhere in the middle with my galley ship model.

The portfolio tracking software I use shows a weighted average dividend yield of 5.3% right now:

Also, my portfolio's beta is about 2/3rds that of the S&P 500 (SPY), which fits my personal tolerance for volatility.

The yield on cost is another important metric, because all else being equal, a buy-and-hold dividend growth portfolio should see its YoC rise over time even as the weighted average dividend yield (based on market valuations) remains stable.

On the right, you can see that my portfolio's dividend growth has averaged 5.5% over the last 5 years, although last year it dropped to about 4%.

That is purely what I'm calling "intrinsic" dividend growth, or the aggregate of dividend hikes from my portfolio holdings. It doesn't include reinvested dividends or new cash I contribute to my account from unspent labor income.

If I only invested in dividend growth stocks, 5.5% weighted average dividend growth would be unimpressive. But keep in mind that this is an average of both the rowers (dividend growers) and sails (high-yielders) in my portfolio.

Portfolio Breakdown

Regular readers know that my sphere of proficiency is real estate, because that was my work background before becoming a full-time financial writer.

I think it makes sense to only invest in individual stocks that are within one's sphere of competency, and preferably also within one's sphere of proficiency. If I own shares of an individual company, I want to treat that like being a minority shareholder in a business (because that's literally what it is), not like a day trader or a chart technician.

I don't always buy and hold individual stocks for a long period of time, but I want to maintain buying discipline such that I would only ever own individual stocks that I'd be willing to own for a long period of time -- as long as the fundamentals hold up.

Also, I have found that it is easiest to hold shares of a stock that is down or dropping when I'm proficient in its industry, business model, balance sheet strength, management skill and shareholder alignment, dividend safety, growth prospects, and so on. I would always prefer to be the "strong hands."

Hence why real estate makes up almost half of my total portfolio.

Note that the pie chart above doesn't quite add up to 100%, because I left out the sectors that have less than a 2% weighting.

Also note that the "Real Estate" sector category above includes both REIT common stocks and REIT preferred stocks.

Lastly, note that this sector breakdown includes the holdings of all ETFs and CEFs that I own. I don't hold any individual stocks in the staples, technology, industrials, or healthcare sectors, but those sectors are represented via the ETFs I own.

Here's a different portfolio breakdown into categories that I consider more functional:

HOLDING CATEGORY Percentage of Portfolio
REITs 40.0%
Leveraged Lenders (BDCs & mREITs) 12.5%
Midstream Energy 11.0%
Preferred Stocks & Baby Bonds 10.5%
Dividend Growth ETFs 10.0%
Renewable Power Producers 8.6%
Closed-End Funds 4.3%
Covered Call ETFs 1.6%
Cash Equivalents 1.5%

Even after the rousing speech above about "investing in what you know," it strikes me that my portfolio is pretty heavy in REITs. More on that below.

Otherwise, with this categorization, you can see that my portfolio contains most of the usual suspects found in income-oriented portfolios: BDCs, midstream energy (including an MLP), preferred stocks, baby bonds, CEFs, and a few covered call ETFs.

REIT Holdings

Last week, I promised a few commenters that I'd share my concentrated, high-quality, high-conviction, 12-member REIT portfolio, and here it is:

REIT Holding Percentage of Portfolio Dividend Yield
Agree Realty (ADC) 8.8% 4.3%
EastGroup Properties (EGP) 5.0% 3.4%
CareTrust REIT (CTRE) 4.1% 3.6%
VICI Properties (VICI) 3.8% 6.3%
American Tower (AMT) 3.7% 3.8%
Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA) 3.6% 4.5%
Healthpeak Properties (DOC) 2.8% 6.8%
American Homes 4 Rent (AMH) 2.0% 3.7%
Public Storage (PSA) 1.7% 4.2%
InvenTrust Properties (IVT) 1.5% 3.2%
Regency Centers (REG) 0.5% 4.2%
American Healthcare REIT (AHR) 0.1% 2.1%

These are by no means the only high-quality REITs out there, in my view. But I view these 12 as a good representative sampling of the high-quality commercial real estate.

By "high-quality" here, I mean well-located and highly desirable real estate, low-leveraged and well-structured balance sheets, top-tier management teams, strong costs of capital, and good growth prospects.

My largest holding of ADC exemplifies this. ADC boasts:

  • Well-located and fungible single-tenant net lease buildings leased overwhelmingly to the strongest retailers in the nation.
  • A motivated and thought-leading management team led by CEO Joey Agree.
  • Significant insider ownership, including many large insider purchases over the last few years.
  • A consistently low-leveraged balance sheet with no material debt maturities until 2028.
  • A monthly dividend that is likely to grow in the mid-single-digits annually for the foreseeable future.

ADC checks all the boxes that a blue-chip compounder should check, which is why it commands such weight in my portfolio.

I could say much the same about EGP's disciplined focus on last-mile multi-tenant industrial parks in the Sunbelt, and CTRE's explosive and yet low-leveraged acquisition-led growth in the senior housing space, and VICI's irreplaceable casino resort properties backed by incredibly well-structured master leases.

I could talk about the scale benefits enjoyed by AMT and MAA, both of which are the leaders in their respective industries.

I could go on at length about how DOC's healthcare portfolio, in spite of past missteps, is poised to greatly benefit from aging demographics, growth in outpatient care, and the spinoff of its senior housing assets.

Meanwhile, in my view, AMH is the best-in-class single-family rental REIT, while PSA is at the very least one of the best self-storage operators in the country -- and definitely the lowest leveraged.

IVT and REG are both high-quality, low-leveraged, grocery-anchored retail REITs with solid growth prospects and recession-resistant portfolios.

And finally, AHR is extremely well-positioned in the senior housing space, as I argued in "Welltower Vs. American Healthcare REIT: Why The Latter Is The Better Buy Today." It yields only a little over 2% today, but I foresee double-digit growth for at least the next few years.

BDC Holdings

I'm also a fan of the BDC business model, but given the heavy use of debt, I exclusively stick to what I view as the highest quality names in this space. All but one of them are internally managed, and that isn't coincidental. Internal management structures generally result in far better alignment between management and shareholders.

Another reason to stick with the higher quality names is the fact that middle-market borrowers are often themselves heavily leveraged, and good loan underwriting in this space involves skill and some degree of risk-aversion.

Yet another reason to go with the creme de la creme of the BDC world is that they tend to trade at high premiums to net asset value ("NAV"), which allows them to issue low-cost equity and keep their leverage relatively low.

With that preamble, here are my BDCs:

BDC Holding Percentage of Portfolio Dividend Yield
Main Street Capital (MAIN) 2.3% 4.9%
Capital Southwest (CSWC) 1.5% 10.0%
Ares Capital (ARCC) 1.0% 9.2%
Trinity Capital (TRIN) 0.9% 12.2%
Hercules Capital (HTGC) 0.9% 8.5%

If these five names hadn't traded at such rich premiums to NAV in recent years, I would have happily continued to buy more shares. But alas, I just haven't seen them as being a good value, and their valuations have generally continued to rise.

MAIN has been my best performing holding ever. I'm up 75% on my cost basis, which obviously doesn't include dividends. I'd love to have a larger position in it, but Mr. Market just hasn't given me a chance to buy shares at what I would deem a reasonable valuation anytime recently.

ARCC is the sole externally managed BDC in my portfolio, but it is managed by Ares Management (ARES), which I consider the industry leader in the private credit space. ARCC has an excellent track record and has only reduced its regular dividend once, during the Great Financial Crisis.

Fixed Income Holdings

One of my favorite kinds of "sails" (high-yielders) is preferred stocks. They typically offer higher yields than corporate bonds from issuers of equivalent credit ratings, and buying below par offers some upside if the security is redeemed.

Preferred Stock / Baby Bond Percentage of Portfolio Dividend Yield
Virtus InfraCap US Preferred ETF (PFFA) 2.0% 9.5%
CTO Realty Growth Series A (CTO.PR.A) 1.1% 7.5%
Arbor Realty Trust Fixed-to-Floating Series F (ABR.PR.F) 1.1% 7.1%
Innovative Industrial Properties Series A (IIPR.PR.A) 1.1% 9.7%
Armada Hoffler Properties (AHH.PR.A) 1.0% 8.0%
Gladstone Land Series B (LANDO) 0.9% 7.5%
Rexford Industrial Properties Series B (REXR.PR.B) 0.7% 6.5%
Rexford Industrial Properties Series C (REXR.PR.C) 0.6% 6.6%
American Homes 4 Rent Series G (AMH.PR.G) 0.5% 6.4%
Regency Centers Series B (REGCO) 0.5% 6.6%
Kimco Realty Series M (KIM.PR.M) 0.4% 6.3%
BIP Bermuda Holdings Sub Notes (BIPI) 0.2% 7.6%
Brookfield Finance Sub Notes (BNJ) 0.2% 7.2%
Brookfield BRP Holdings Sub Notes (BEPH) 0.2% 7.6%

Note that I've included PFFA, a preferred stock ETF, in this category as well as the individual preferred stocks and baby bonds.

As you can see, the preferreds from sub-investment grade issuers like CTO Realty Growth (CTO), Arbor Realty (ABR), Innovative Industrial Properties (IIPR), Armada Hoffler Properties (AHH), and Gladstone Land (LAND) top the list with the largest weightings. These are strategic bets on my part. I think the preferred dividends are safe despite no desire whatsoever to own the common stocks of these companies.

Meanwhile, the middle of the pack is made up of preferreds from investment grade issuers like Rexford Industrial Properties (REXR), American Homes 4 Rent, Regency Centers, and Kimco Realty (KIM). I liked the 6%+ yield from these high-quality REIT issuers, two of which I own as common stock positions as well.

Finally, I've got small bets on three baby bonds issued by various subsidiaries of Brookfield Corporation (BN). The financials of these subsidiaries are complex and opaque, which is why they trade at such high yields and discounts to par. But I find the risk-reward profile compelling enough for a small bet.

Top 5 Holdings

You can begin to piece together my top holdings from the above data, but I'll go ahead and lay out my top 5 holdings here.

Company Percentage of Portfolio
1. Agree Realty (ADC) 8.8%
2. Clearway Energy Inc. (CWEN.A) 6.0%
3. EastGroup Properties (EGP) 5.0%
4. Hannon Armstrong Sustainable Infra. (HASI) 5.0%
5. Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) 4.5%

I share my top 10 holdings and all my trades as part of my monthly portfolio updates at High Yield Landlord, so I won't go further than my top 5 holdings in this article.

I've already shared some brief thoughts on ADC and EGP above, so what about the other three?

CWEN owns and operates power production facilities, mostly renewables like wind, solar, and battery storage but also some natural gas plants. Although CWEN does not have an investment grade credit rating, the balance sheet is structured well, and management is able to secure high enough cash yields on investments to maintain a robust spread over CWEN's cost of capital. The company's growth runway looks very strong due to the incredible demand for power from data centers. Hence the recently announced 1.2 gigawatt power purchase agreement with Google (GOOG, GOOGL).

HASI enjoys the same secular tailwinds as CWEN, because HASI is a financier and structured equity investor in various green energy projects across the country. Its business model looks a lot like that of a BDC, but its credit losses over time have been extremely low — lower than any public BDC.

Lastly, EPD is a high-quality master limited partnership in the midstream space with low leverage, a vast and competitively advantageous infrastructure portfolio, and heavy insider ownership. The founding Duncan family still own almost 1/3rd of the company and, in my view, are a major reason for EPD's financial conservatism.

Long-Term Target Portfolio

As I've explained in recent articles, it has become a goal of mine to incrementally lean into dividend growth (rowers) over yield (sails).

If and when I have kids, I don't envision myself being quite as gung-ho about individual stock research and turning over every stone in search of gold. Plus, my portfolio has become increasingly concentrated in real estate, probably too much so even for someone whose specialty is real estate.

I have no interest in selling or trimming any of my REITs at this time. In fact, I'm going to keep selectively buying more shares of certain REITs. But I do want to gradually transition my portfolio toward passive investments over time.

HOLDING CATEGORY Current Share of Portfolio Target Share of Portfolio

Funds (ETFs & CEFs)

16%

 40%
REITs 40% 20%
Leveraged Lenders (Including BDCs) 12.5% 10%
Midstream Energy 11% 10%
Fixed Income (Prefs & Baby Bonds) 10.5% 10%
Renewable Power Producers 8.6% 5%
Cash Equivalents 1.5% 5%

Eventually (I don't know how long it will take), I'd like to have at least 40% of my portfolio in various funds — mostly dividend growth ETFs but also some CEFs and covered call ETFs.

The "Leveraged Lenders," "Midstream Energy," and "Fixed Income" categories are already pretty close to my target share, so the goal going forward will simply be to more or less maintain that share — as long as market prices allow me to do so.

My two holdings in the "Renewable Power Producers" category, CWEN.A and Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP), together command plenty of weight in my portfolio, and I don't foresee myself adding to those positions anytime soon.

Lastly, my cash position is lower than I would like it to be right now. Keep in mind this excludes my personal "rainy day" savings. This is purely investable cash. I'm fine with a slight drag on my portfolio performance during bull markets to have dry powder during market selloffs.

My takeaway here is that I need to make a concerted effort to increase my incremental buying of dividend growth ETFs and cash equivalent vehicles.

My Buy List Today

Keep in mind that I'm still in the accumulation phase of life and infusing new cash into my account every month. Between accrued dividends and depositing unspent labor income into my brokerage account, I am regularly buying more shares of something to grow the passive income snowball.

Here's my current buy list:
